message.content:
Read `./.claude/progress.txt`, pick the most important pending tasks, execute it, update the file, commit, and stop.

'./.claude/progress.txt' is:
- your persistent memory.
- Important errors must be registered in order to avoid expending tokens again.
- Never run tasks not registered in it.
- Register new tasks if needed, respecting the current structure.


WORKFLOW:
0. Understand Project Facts from CLAUDE.md
1. Read ./.claude/progress.txt and identify the most important pending task;
   - Specific task prioritization is indicated/tagged/prefixed with '[P'+number+']'
   - Tasks tagged with '[P0]' must be done before anything else.
   - Tasks tagged with P bigger than 999 (e.g. '[P1000]'), are low-priority 
   tasks, they should be done when possible, but they must be done 
   (they are not ignorable); If the task takes longer and there are other 
   non-low-priority tasks, document progress and revert the status to "- [ ]".
   
2. If NO pending tasks exist, signal completion (see OUTPUT STATUS below) and stop
3. If there are tasks, mark it as IN_PROGRESS and then sequentially run:
   a. Evaluate scope: if the task involves more than ~3 files or multiple concerns,
      break it into smaller sub-tasks in progress.txt BEFORE starting. Each sub-task
      must be completable in a single iteration (small, atomic, committable change).
   b. Execute task (respecting current project structure and ./.claude/STYLE_GUIDE.md)
   c. Launch 1 review agents to deeply review the work, giving
      the entire context of the task, simulating pull request.
   d. Review output and consider improvements
   e. Implement corrections if needed
   f. Update progress.txt marking task as completed
   g. Commit task changes (not include unrelated code)
   h. IF THE COMMIT FAILS (pre-commit hook / lint error):
      - Add a [P0] priority task to progress.txt describing the exact errors
      - Retry the commit with `git commit --no-verify` to save your work
      - The NEXT iteration will fix the lint errors as its top priority
   i. Output status block (see below)
   j. Stop

- Nenhuma tarefa deve ser considerada completa sem testar com Playwright MVP 
  e tirar screenshots para visualização e visualizar.
  
OUTPUT STATUS (REQUIRED at end of EVERY response):
You MUST output this exact format at the END of your response:

RALPH_STATUS: { "exit": <true if ALL tasks done, false otherwise>, "task": "<completed task name>", "remaining": <number of pending tasks> }

Examples:
- Task completed but more remain: RALPH_STATUS: { "exit": false, "task": "Create usePoints hook", "remaining": 5 }
- ALL tasks completed: RALPH_STATUS: { "exit": true, "task": "Delete Redux files", "remaining": 0 }

CRITICAL (THINK BEFORE ACTING):
1. The FIRST thing you MUST do is write your understanding of each WORKFLOW statement
2. The SECOND thing you MUST do is write your understanding of the work in progress described in progress.txt
3. Only AFTER completing steps 1 and 2, proceed with the task execution
4. If more work is necessary, add a new task to the file, set "exit": false, and Stop
5. Only set "exit": true when ALL tasks in progress.txt are completed
6. For @semparar/* packages, read the real js code because some types are not updated correctly.
7. Never remove tasks, unless to update status;
8. Never include workarounds and add tasks to fix all you found ('as any' for example is a workaround)

# SP_0002.0 — SDUI Core: Types & Interfaces

      .claude/rfc-conteudo-dinamico.txt (sections 3.1–3.5).
  > Types at src/services/sdui/models/index.ts (180 lines).

  >   SDUIConditional, SDUIValidation, SDUIComponentRegistry, SDUIComponentRenderer.
  > SDUI_KNOWN_TYPES const array with all 13 voucher-relevant component types.

Context:
- Screen structure: { id, context, header, body[], footer } (RFC section 3.1)
- Components: { type, props } (RFC section 3.2)
- Rich text: { type: "rich_text", children: [nodes] } (RFC section 3.3)
- Context interpolation: {{var}} pattern (RFC section 3.4)
- Conditionals: JSON Logic subset (RFC section 3.5)
- Follow project conventions: .claude/STYLE_GUIDE.md (services architecture,
  models in Models/ folders)

entire SDUI system (Screen, Component, Context, RichText, ComponentRegistry).

# SP_0002.1 — SDUI Core: Interpolation Parser
- [DONE] Implement the {{var}} replacement engine from RFC section 3.4.
  > src/services/sdui/utils/interpolation.ts — 3 functions:
  >   resolveContextPath (nested path + array index support)
  >   interpolateString ({{var}} regex replacement)
  >   interpolateDeep (recursive object/array walk)
  > 24 unit tests passing (interpolation.test.ts).
  > Storybook demo at src/__stories__/SDUIInterpolation.stories.tsx.
  > Screenshot validated via Playwright.

Context:
- Must resolve nested paths: {{product.name}}, {{balance.pointsFormatted}}
- Must deep-walk an object interpolating all string values in props
- Missing values should resolve gracefully (empty string, not crash)
- Existing interpolation example in RFC:
  "Voucher para {{product.name}}" + context.product.name = "Abastecimento"
  → "Voucher para Abastecimento"
- Types from SP_0002.0 should be used

Deliverable: Interpolation utility with unit tests.

# SP_0002.2 — SDUI Core: Component Registry & Screen Renderer
- [DONE] Build the rendering engine that turns a JSON screen definition into React
      components. This is the core of the SDUI architecture (RFC sections 3.1–3.2).
  > Registry at src/services/sdui/registry/index.ts — createRegistry, registerComponent,
  >   getRenderer, registerToDefault (immutable API + mutable default singleton).
  > ScreenRenderer at src/services/sdui/renderer/ScreenRenderer.tsx — renders header,
  >   body[], footer from SDUIScreen JSON. Applies interpolateDeep to all props.
  > FallbackComponent at src/services/sdui/renderer/FallbackComponent.tsx — visible
  >   yellow box for unknown component types (uses RN primitives for ThemeProvider safety).
  > 14 unit tests passing (registry.test.ts + ScreenRenderer.test.tsx).
  > Storybook demo at src/__stories__/SDUIScreenRenderer.stories.tsx.
  > Screenshot validated via Playwright.

Context:
- Registry maps string type → React component (RFC section 3.2 "Componentes")
- Renderer receives SDUIScreen JSON, iterates body[], renders each via registry
- Must apply interpolation (SP_0002.1) to all string props using screen context
- Unknown types should render a visible fallback (not crash)
- Types from SP_0002.0, interpolation from SP_0002.1

screen definition from JSON.

================================================================================
=== PHASE 6: RFC MISSING IMPLEMENTATIONS ======================================
================================================================================

NOTE: These tasks implement RFC features that have types defined (SP_0002.0)
but no runtime code. They complete the SDUI engine to full RFC spec.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.0 — JSON Logic Evaluator
- [ ] Implement the JSON Logic rule evaluator described in RFC section 3.5.

Context:
- RFC defines operators: var (data access), ==, !=, >, <, >=, <= (comparison),
  and, or, ! (logical)
- Types already exist: SDUIRule, SDUIComparisonRule, SDUILogicalRule,
  SDUIVarOperator, SDUIConditional (in src/services/sdui/models/index.ts)
- Needs a function: evaluateRule(rule: SDUIRule, context: SDUIContext): boolean
- var operator uses resolveContextPath from interpolation.ts
- Invalid/malformed rules should return false (RFC 6.4: "Regras inválidas
  retornam false")
- For `visible` conditionals specifically: invalid rules should default to
  true (show component) — hiding content by default on bad data is worse UX
- Must be pure function, no side effects
- Reference: https://jsonlogic.com/

Deliverable: src/services/sdui/utils/jsonLogic.ts with evaluateRule() +
comprehensive unit tests covering all operators and edge cases.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.1 — Component Visibility & Button Enabled Conditionals
- [ ] Wire the JSON Logic evaluator into ScreenRenderer for component
      visibility and button enabled state.

Context:
- SDUIComponent already has optional `visible: SDUIConditional` field
- ScreenRenderer currently ignores `visible` — should skip rendering when
  evaluateRule returns false
- ButtonAdapter should process `enabled: { rule: SDUIRule }` in props —
  disable button when rule evaluates to false
- Depends on: SP_0006.0 (evaluateRule)
- Update existing ScreenRenderer.test.tsx and adapters.test.tsx
- Add Storybook demo showing conditional visibility toggle

Deliverable: Components with `visible` conditionals are hidden/shown based on
context. Buttons with `enabled` rules are disabled/enabled dynamically.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.2 — Input Validation Rules
- [ ] Implement validation rule execution for SDUI input components.

Context:
- RFC section 3.5 defines validation pattern:
  { "validation": { "rules": [{ "rule": {...}, "message": "error text" }] } }
- Types exist: SDUIValidationRule, SDUIValidation
- PointsInputAdapter has textError prop but no validation integration
- Needs: validateField(value, validation: SDUIValidation, context): string | null
  (returns first failing rule's message, or null if valid)
- Depends on: SP_0006.0 (evaluateRule)
- Should integrate with PointsInputAdapter's error display
- Scope: validation utility function only. Form-level orchestration (when to
  trigger: blur/change/submit) is a separate concern for action handlers
- Add Storybook demo showing validation in action

Deliverable: src/services/sdui/utils/validation.ts with validateField() +
integration into PointsInputAdapter + unit tests.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.3 — Rich Text Adapter
- [ ] Implement the rich_text component adapter described in RFC section 3.3.

Context:
- RFC defines rich text as array of typed nodes:
  - text: { type: "text", value: string, bold?: boolean, italic?: boolean, color?: string }
  - link: { type: "link", text: string, to: string, trackEvent?: string }
  - icon: { type: "icon", name: string, color?: string, size?: number }
  - linebreak: { type: "linebreak" }
- Types already defined: SDUIRichText, SDUITextNode, SDUILinkNode, SDUIIconNode,
  SDUILineBreakNode (in models/index.ts)
- Should render inline — all nodes in a single wrapping Text component
  (React Native supports nested Text for inline formatting)
- Links should use DS Text with underline style (similar to LinkAdapter)
- Icons use DS Icon component inline
- Linebreak nodes render as `\n` inside Text (not DOM br element —
  React Native Text supports newline characters)
- Register as "rich_text" type in adapter registry
- Add mock payload using rich_text to test inline formatting

Deliverable: RichTextAdapter registered in SDUI registry + unit tests +
Storybook demo showing mixed bold/link/icon inline text.

You are a senior code reviewer simulating a pull request review. Review the following two files for correctness, edge cases, security, and code quality:

1. src/services/sdui/utils/jsonLogic.ts — JSON Logic evaluator implementing RFC section 3.5
2. src/services/sdui/utils/jsonLogic.test.ts — 34 unit tests

Context:
- This implements a JSON Logic rule evaluator for an SDUI (Server Driven UI) engine
- It supports: var (data access), ==, !=, >, <, >=, <= (comparison), and, or, ! (logical)
- Types are defined in src/services/sdui/models/index.ts
- resolveContextPath from interpolation.ts returns '' for missing paths (not null)
- Invalid/malformed rules should return false (RFC 6.4)
- Must be pure function, no side effects

Read both files and provide:
1. Any bugs or correctness issues
2. Missing edge cases in tests
3. Security concerns (e.g., prototype pollution, injection)
4. Code quality suggestions
5. Overall verdict: APPROVE or REQUEST_CHANGES

content.text:
---

## Pull Request Review: `jsonLogic.ts` + `jsonLogic.test.ts`

### 1. Bugs / Correctness Issues

**BUG-1: Ordering comparison operators on non-numeric types produce silent nonsense (Medium)**

Lines 54-61 in `jsonLogic.ts`:
```ts
case '>':
  return (left as number) > (right as number);
```

The `as number` cast is cosmetic -- at runtime, if `left` is `'abc'` and `right` is `null`, JS will evaluate `'abc' > null` which coerces to `NaN > 0` producing `false`. While the RFC says "invalid rules return false," this is accidental correctness, not intentional. If someone passes `{ '>': ['z', 'a'] }`, it returns `true` via string comparison, which may or may not be desired. This needs an explicit design decision: either guard with a numeric type check (return `false` if either operand is not a number) or document that string ordering is intentionally supported.

**Recommendation:** Add a type guard before ordering comparisons:
```ts
case '>':
  if (typeof left !== 'number' || typeof right !== 'number') return false;
  return left > right;
```

**BUG-2: `resolveContextPath` returns `''` for missing paths, but `evaluateRule` treats `''` as a resolved value (Low-Medium)**

This creates a semantic mismatch. The test on line 48 asserts `{ '==': [{ var: 'nonexistent.path' }, ''] }` is `true`, which is correct given the current behavior. However, this means you cannot distinguish between "field is missing" and "field is literally empty string." This is documented behavior per the RFC mention in the test, but it is worth calling out as a known limitation that could cause subtle logic bugs in production rules.

**BUG-3: `!` (negation) operator does not validate its operand (Medium)**

Line 87:
```ts
if (operator === '!') {
  return !evaluateRule(operands as SDUIRule, context);
}
```

If `operands` is an array (e.g., `{ '!': [{ '==': [1, 1] }] }`), the cast `operands as SDUIRule` passes an array into `evaluateRule`. `evaluateRule` then hits `Object.keys(rule)` on an array, which returns index strings `['0']`, and `operator` becomes `'0'`, which falls through to `return false`. So `{ '!': [someRule] }` returns `!false === true` regardless of the inner rule. This is a real bug -- standard JSON Logic accepts `{ '!': [rule] }` with a single-element array.

**Fix:**
```ts
if (operator === '!') {
  const inner = Array.isArray(operands) ? operands[0] : operands;
  return !evaluateRule(inner as SDUIRule, context);
}
```

**Add test:**
```ts
it('!: negation with array syntax', () => {
  expect(evaluateRule({ '!': [{ '==': [1, 1] }] } as SDUIRule, CONTEXT)).toBe(false);
  expect(evaluateRule({ '!': [{ '==': [1, 2] }] } as SDUIRule, CONTEXT)).toBe(true);
});
```

---

### 2. Missing Edge Cases in Tests

| Missing scenario | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| `!` with array operand (see BUG-3) | Standard JSON Logic allows `{ "!": [rule] }` |
| Comparison with `null` on ordering operators (`{ '>': [null, 5] }`) | `null > 5` is `false` in JS but through implicit coercion, not intentional logic |
| Comparison with `string` on ordering operators (`{ '>': ['z', 'a'] }`) | Accidentally works via string comparison |
| `or` / `and` with non-rule elements (`{ and: [true, false] }`) | Primitives passed as sub-rules to `evaluateRule` -- both return `false` due to the `typeof rule !== 'object'` guard, so `{ and: [true] }` returns `false`, which may surprise |
| Rule with multiple keys (`{ '==': [1,1], '>': [5,3] }`) | Only the first key is evaluated; second is silently ignored. Worth a test to document this behavior |
| Deeply nested `var` path traversal through arrays (`{ var: 'items.0.name' }`) | `resolveContextPath` supports array indexing but there is no test for it through `evaluateRule` |
| Very deep nesting / recursion | No stack overflow protection. A malicious backend could craft a deeply nested rule tree |
| `{ var: '' }` (empty string path) | Edge case for `resolveContextPath` -- would `split('.')` produce `['']` |

---

### 3. Security Concerns

**SEC-1: No prototype pollution guard in `resolveContextPath` (Medium)**

File `/Users/anotonio.silva/dev/rnm-app-fintech-rewardprogram/src/services/sdui/utils/interpolation.ts`, line 21:
```ts
current = (current as SDUIContext)[segment];
```

If the backend sends a `var` path like `constructor.prototype` or `__proto__`, this does a raw property lookup on the object. While the context objects are plain data (not class instances), accessing `__proto__` on any JS object returns `Object.prototype`, which could leak information or cause unexpected behavior if the result is then used in comparisons.

**Recommendation:** Add a denylist check in `resolveContextPath`:
```ts
const DENIED_KEYS = new Set(['__proto__', 'constructor', 'prototype']);
if (DENIED_KEYS.has(segment)) return '';
```

**SEC-2: No recursion depth limit (Low-Medium)**

`evaluateRule` recurses through `and`, `or`, `!`, and nested comparison operands with no depth limit. A malicious SDUI payload could craft a rule 10,000 levels deep and blow the call stack. In practice, the backend is trusted, but defense-in-depth says add a `MAX_DEPTH` counter (e.g., 20).

**SEC-3: `resolveContextPath` does not use `hasOwnProperty` (Low)**

Line 21 of `interpolation.ts` accesses properties without checking `hasOwnProperty`. This means inherited properties (like `toString`, `valueOf`) are accessible via `var` paths. For example, `{ var: 'product.toString' }` would resolve to the native `toString` function, which `isPrimitive` would reject (returning `null`), so the practical risk is low, but it is still worth noting.

---

### 4. Code Quality Suggestions

**QA-1: The `eslint-disable-line eqeqeq` comments on lines 51-53 are justified** -- loose equality is intentional per JSON Logic spec. Good use of inline disabling rather than project-wide rule changes.

**QA-2: `evaluateComparison` takes `operator: string` but could take a narrower union type.**
```ts
type ComparisonOp = '==' | '!=' | '>' | '<' | '>=' | '<=';
```
This would eliminate the `default: return false` branch and let TypeScript enforce exhaustiveness.

**QA-3: The `comparisonOps` array on line 90 is rebuilt on every call.** Move it to module scope as a `Set` for O(1) lookup:
```ts
const COMPARISON_OPS = new Set(['==', '!=', '>', '<', '>=', '<=']);
```

**QA-4: `resolveOperand` line 38 casts `operand as SDUIRule` without validation.** If an operand is an object that is neither a `var` operator nor a valid rule, it silently recurses into `evaluateRule` which returns `false`. This is fine per RFC 6.4, but a comment explaining this deliberate fallthrough would improve readability.

**QA-5: Test organization is clean.** The separation into `var operator`, `comparison operators`, `logical operators`, `nested logic`, and `edge cases` sections is well structured. The context fixture is reusable and representative.

---

### 5. Summary Table

| Category | Finding | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| Bug | `!` operator does not handle array syntax | Medium -- breaks JSON Logic compat |
| Bug | Ordering ops silently accept non-numeric types | Medium -- accidental correctness |
| Bug | Missing path vs empty string conflation | Low -- documented but risky |
| Security | No `__proto__`/`constructor` guard in path resolution | Medium |
| Security | No recursion depth limit | Low-Medium |
| Test gap | `!` with array operand not tested | High -- masks BUG-3 |
| Test gap | Non-numeric ordering comparisons not tested | Medium |
| Test gap | Primitives inside `and`/`or` arrays not tested | Low |
| Quality | `comparisonOps` array rebuilt per call | Low |
| Quality | `operator` param could use union type | Low |

---

### Verdict: **REQUEST_CHANGES**

The `!` operator array-syntax bug (BUG-3) is a correctness issue that will surface the moment a backend sends the standard JSON Logic form `{ "!": [rule] }` instead of `{ "!": rule }`. The prototype pollution vector in `resolveContextPath` is also a meaningful security gap for a system that evaluates server-provided payloads.

**Required changes before approval:**
1. Fix `!` operator to handle both `{ "!": rule }` and `{ "!": [rule] }` forms, with corresponding tests.
2. Add `__proto__` / `constructor` / `prototype` denylist in `resolveContextPath`.
3. Add a recursion depth limit (suggest 20) to `evaluateRule`.

**Recommended (non-blocking):**
4. Add type guard for numeric comparisons in ordering operators.
5. Hoist `COMPARISON_OPS` to module scope as a `Set`.
6. Add tests for the missing edge cases listed above.

content.text:
The depth test is failing because 25 levels of `!` negation with `MAX_DEPTH = 20` doesn't return false — it returns the result at depth 21 which hits the guard. Let me trace: the outer evaluate starts at depth 0, each `!` increments by 1, so depth 25 would indeed exceed 20. But the issue is that with 25 negations wrapping `{ '==': [1, 1] }`, the inner comparison is at depth 25, which exceeds 20, returning false. Then 25 negations of false: odd number of negations of false = true. The depth guard returns false, then `!false = true, !true = false, !false = true...` — so it alternates. Let me just make the nesting deeper to ensure we hit the guard before any valid evaluation.

Actually, let me reconsider. The depth check returns `false` at depth > 20. Then the `!` at depth 20 gets `false` from the guard, negates it to `true`. That propagates up through alternating negations. The key issue is that `false` from the depth guard looks the same as `false` from a failed rule.

The test expectation is wrong — the result of exceeding depth depends on how many negation layers are above the cutoff. Let me fix the test to use `and` wrapping which won't negate the false.

================================================================================
=== PHASE 6: RFC MISSING IMPLEMENTATIONS ======================================
================================================================================

NOTE: These tasks implement RFC features that have types defined (SP_0002.0)
but no runtime code. They complete the SDUI engine to full RFC spec.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.0 — JSON Logic Evaluator
- [ ] Implement the JSON Logic rule evaluator described in RFC section 3.5.

Context:
- RFC defines operators: var (data access), ==, !=, >, <, >=, <= (comparison),
  and, or, ! (logical)
- Types already exist: SDUIRule, SDUIComparisonRule, SDUILogicalRule,
  SDUIVarOperator, SDUIConditional (in src/services/sdui/models/index.ts)
- Needs a function: evaluateRule(rule: SDUIRule, context: SDUIContext): boolean
- var operator uses resolveContextPath from interpolation.ts
- Invalid/malformed rules should return false (RFC 6.4: "Regras inválidas
  retornam false")
- For `visible` conditionals specifically: invalid rules should default to
  true (show component) — hiding content by default on bad data is worse UX
- Must be pure function, no side effects
- Reference: https://jsonlogic.com/

Deliverable: src/services/sdui/utils/jsonLogic.ts with evaluateRule() +
comprehensive unit tests covering all operators and edge cases.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.1 — Component Visibility & Button Enabled Conditionals
- [ ] Wire the JSON Logic evaluator into ScreenRenderer for component
      visibility and button enabled state.

Context:
- SDUIComponent already has optional `visible: SDUIConditional` field
- ScreenRenderer currently ignores `visible` — should skip rendering when
  evaluateRule returns false
- ButtonAdapter should process `enabled: { rule: SDUIRule }` in props —
  disable button when rule evaluates to false
- Depends on: SP_0006.0 (evaluateRule)
- Update existing ScreenRenderer.test.tsx and adapters.test.tsx
- Add Storybook demo showing conditional visibility toggle

Deliverable: Components with `visible` conditionals are hidden/shown based on
context. Buttons with `enabled` rules are disabled/enabled dynamically.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.2 — Input Validation Rules
- [ ] Implement validation rule execution for SDUI input components.

Context:
- RFC section 3.5 defines validation pattern:
  { "validation": { "rules": [{ "rule": {...}, "message": "error text" }] } }
- Types exist: SDUIValidationRule, SDUIValidation
- PointsInputAdapter has textError prop but no validation integration
- Needs: validateField(value, validation: SDUIValidation, context): string | null
  (returns first failing rule's message, or null if valid)
- Depends on: SP_0006.0 (evaluateRule)
- Should integrate with PointsInputAdapter's error display
- Scope: validation utility function only. Form-level orchestration (when to
  trigger: blur/change/submit) is a separate concern for action handlers
- Add Storybook demo showing validation in action

Deliverable: src/services/sdui/utils/validation.ts with validateField() +
integration into PointsInputAdapter + unit tests.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# SP_0006.3 — Rich Text Adapter
- [ ] Implement the rich_text component adapter described in RFC section 3.3.

Context:
- RFC defines rich text as array of typed nodes:
  - text: { type: "text", value: string, bold?: boolean, italic?: boolean, color?: string }
  - link: { type: "link", text: string, to: string, trackEvent?: string }
  - icon: { type: "icon", name: string, color?: string, size?: number }
  - linebreak: { type: "linebreak" }
- Types already defined: SDUIRichText, SDUITextNode, SDUILinkNode, SDUIIconNode,
  SDUILineBreakNode (in models/index.ts)
- Should render inline — all nodes in a single wrapping Text component
  (React Native supports nested Text for inline formatting)
- Links should use DS Text with underline style (similar to LinkAdapter)
- Icons use DS Icon component inline
- Linebreak nodes render as `\n` inside Text (not DOM br element —
  React Native Text supports newline characters)
- Register as "rich_text" type in adapter registry
- Add mock payload using rich_text to test inline formatting

Deliverable: RichTextAdapter registered in SDUI registry + unit tests +
Storybook demo showing mixed bold/link/icon inline text.
